questions.question:
Modern warfare has changed from large scale clashes of armies to suppression of civilian populations. Chemical agents that do their work silently appear to be suited to such warfare; and regretfully, there exist people in military establishments who think that chemical agents are useful tools for their cause.

Which of the following statements best sums up the meaning of the above passage?

A. Modern warfare has resulted in civil strife.
B. Chemical agents are useful in modern warfare.
C. Use of chemical agents in modern warfare would be undesirable.
D. People in military establishments like to use chemical agents in war.
questions.answer: D. People in military establishments like to use chemical agents in war.
questions.to_find: Statement that best sums up the meaning of the passage

understand.plain: The passage describes a shift in warfare: from large-scale army conflicts to suppressing civilians. It notes that chemical agents, which work silently, appear suited to this kind of warfare. The author laments that some people in military establishments see chemical agents as useful tools. The key message is that certain military personnel view chemical agents as beneficial weapons.

keywords.explain: Modern warfare targets non-combatants rather than opposing armies
keywords.example: Chemical attacks on towns and villages rather than military fronts

keywords.explain: Substances used as weapons that act silently and can harm large numbers of people
keywords.example: Nerve agents or toxic gases deployed against populations

keywords.explain: The author's tone of disapproval signals that what follows is a negative observation
keywords.example: The author regrets that some military people think chemicals are useful

understand.visual_alt: A passage about modern warfare and chemical agents used against civilian populations

given.aim: Identify which option correctly captures the main point of the passage

terms.meaning: Warfare has moved from army-to-army battles to suppressing civilians
terms.example: Chemical attacks on civilian settlements rather than military engagements
terms.connects: Provides context for why chemical agents are being discussed

terms.meaning: Their silent, indiscriminate nature makes them appear useful for civilian suppression
terms.example: A silent toxin can harm many civilians without warning
terms.connects: Links the nature of chemical agents to modern warfare tactics

terms.meaning: There exist people in military bodies who advocate for chemical agents as tools
terms.example: Defense officials who favour chemical weapons programs
terms.connects: The passage's core claim: some military people think chemical agents are useful

given.plan: Read each option against the passage carefully. The passage's main point is not that chemicals ARE good (option B), nor that warfare caused civil strife (option A), nor even that their use would be undesirable (option C is too normative). The passage states as a fact that people in military establishments think chemical agents are useful tools — option D.

steps.title: Identify the author's central claim
steps.apply: The passage says there exist people in military establishments who think chemical agents are useful tools for their cause. This is stated as a factual observation (even if the author finds it regrettable).
steps.note: The word 'regretfully' shows the author's disapproval but does not change the fact being reported

steps.title: Evaluate each option against the passage
steps.apply: A — warfare resulting in civil strife is not the passage's claim. B — the passage does not assert chemicals ARE useful, only that some people think so. C — the passage implies disapproval but does not make a normative recommendation. D — directly restates the factual observation: people in military establishments like to use chemical agents.
steps.note: The best summary captures the factual statement made in the passage rather than inferring a recommendation

solution.result: D. People in military establishments like to use chemical agents in war.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal passage-reading question; no mathematical formulae are required.

questions.question:
Few school curricula include a unit on how to deal with bereavement and grief, yet all students at some point in their lives suffer from losses through death and parting.

Based on the above passage, which topic would NOT be included in a unit on bereavement?

A. how to write a letter of condolence
B. what emotional stages are passed through in the healing process
C. what the leading causes of death are
D. how to give support to a grieving friend
questions.answer: C. what the leading causes of death are
questions.to_find: Topic that would NOT be included in a unit on bereavement

understand.plain: The passage observes that schools rarely teach students how to cope with bereavement and grief, even though every student eventually experiences loss. A hypothetical unit on bereavement would therefore focus on coping skills, emotional support, and healing — not on statistics or medical causes of death. The question asks us to identify the one topic that falls outside the scope of such a unit.

keywords.explain: The state of grief and loss experienced after the death of a loved one
keywords.example: Mourning the death of a family member or close friend

keywords.explain: The psychological stages a person moves through after a loss, such as denial, anger, acceptance
Expression of sympathy and sorrow to a person who has experienced a loss
keywords.example: Writing a condolence letter to a bereaved family

understand.visual_alt: A passage about school curricula lacking bereavement education

given.aim: Find the one topic that does not belong in a bereavement curriculum unit

terms.meaning: A written expression of sympathy to someone grieving — clearly relevant to supporting the bereaved
terms.example: Writing to a friend who has lost a parent
terms.connects: Directly relevant to coping with and supporting bereavement

terms.meaning: Psychological phases after a loss (denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance)
terms.example: Understanding that sadness and anger are normal reactions to loss
terms.connects: Core content for any bereavement curriculum

terms.meaning: Medical/epidemiological data on why people die (heart disease, cancer, etc.)
terms.connects: This is a medical/public-health topic, not a coping or grief-support topic

given.plan: A bereavement unit focuses on how to cope with loss: writing condolences (A), understanding the emotional healing process (B), supporting grieving friends (D). Knowing the statistical causes of death (C) is irrelevant to learning how to grieve or support others.

steps.title: Define the scope of a bereavement unit
steps.apply: The passage implies a bereavement unit would teach students to handle losses through death and parting — so it covers emotional responses, support skills, and communication around grief.
steps.note: The focus is on coping and interpersonal support, not medical knowledge

steps.title: Eliminate options that belong in a bereavement unit
steps.apply: A (condolence letters) — yes, a coping/communication skill. B (emotional stages of healing) — yes, core grief education. D (supporting a grieving friend) — yes, interpersonal support skill. C (leading causes of death) — a public-health statistic, not relevant to coping with loss.
steps.note: Option C is about why people die, not about how to deal with grief

solution.result: C. what the leading causes of death are

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal passage-reading question; no mathematical formulae are required.

questions.question:
After several defeats in wars, Robert Bruce went into exile and wanted to commit suicide. Just before committing suicide, he came across a spider attempting tirelessly to have its net. Time and again, the spider failed but that did not deter it from making attempts. Such attempts by the spider encouraged Bruce not to commit suicide. And then, Bruce went back again and won a battle.

Which one of the following assertions is best supported by the above information?

A. Failure is the pillar of success.
B. Life ends with all its difficulties.
C. Honesty is the best policy.
D. Life begins after giving up hope.
questions.answer: D. Life begins after giving up hope.
questions.to_find: Assertion best supported by the passage about Robert Bruce and the spider

understand.plain: The passage narrates how Robert Bruce, defeated and suicidal, was inspired by a spider that kept trying to spin its web despite repeated failures. The spider's persistence stopped Bruce from giving up his life, and he went on to win a battle. The lesson drawn is not merely about persistence after failure, but about how life (and success) can begin precisely when a person is at their lowest — after having apparently given up hope.

keywords.explain: Bruce had reached complete despair — he had given up hope after repeated defeats
keywords.example: A person who feels there is no reason to continue living after repeated failures

keywords.explain: The spider kept attempting to spin its net despite failing each time, symbolising never giving up
keywords.example: Trying a difficult task over and over until it succeeds

keywords.explanation: After being inspired, Bruce returned and succeeded — life resumed after the lowest point
keywords.example: A comeback story where someone recovers from total defeat

understand.visual_alt: A passage about Robert Bruce finding inspiration from a spider and returning to win a battle

given.aim: Select the assertion that most accurately captures the moral of the Robert Bruce and spider story

terms.term: Failure is the pillar of success
terms.meaning: Failures build the foundation for eventual success
terms.example: Learning from mistakes to eventually succeed
terms.connects: Partially relevant — Bruce did fail before winning — but the passage's key moment is that Bruce wanted to die (gave up hope), not merely that he failed

terms.term: Life begins after giving up hope
terms.meaning: When a person has reached the point of despair, a new beginning is still possible
terms.example: Bruce was about to end his life but then found new purpose and won
terms.connects: This exactly matches the narrative: Bruce gave up hope (suicidal) and then found a reason to live and go on to succeed

terms.meaning: Ethical conduct is the wisest strategy
terms.example: Being truthful leads to better outcomes

given.plan: The story's turning point is Bruce at his absolute lowest — suicidal and hopeless. The spider inspires him and he wins. Option D captures this: life (and success) literally begins after Bruce had given up hope. Option A (failure as pillar of success) is too generic; the passage emphasises the hopeless state, not just failure.

steps.title: Identify the critical turning point in the story
steps.apply: Bruce had given up entirely — he wanted to commit suicide. The spider's persistence inspired him precisely at the moment of total despair. He then went on to win. The moral is that a new beginning is possible even after total loss of hope.
steps.note: The passage emphasises 'wanted to commit suicide' — complete hopelessness — not just multiple failures

steps.title: Match the moral to the options
steps.apply: A — about failure and success generally, does not capture the hopelessness angle. B — unrelated to the passage. C — about honesty, completely off-topic. D — directly mirrors the story: Bruce had given up hope, the spider inspired him, and life (success) began anew.
steps.note: Option D is the only one that incorporates the key element: having given up hope before the turnaround

solution.result: D. Life begins after giving up hope.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal passage-reading question; no mathematical formulae are required.

questions.question:
The Palghat Gap (or Palakkad Gap), a region about 30 km wide in the southern part of the Western Ghats in India, is lower than the hilly terrain to its north and south. The exact reasons for the formation of this gap are not clear. It results in the neighbouring regions of Kerala getting more rainfall from the South West monsoon and the regions in the neighbouring areas of Tamil Nadu getting less rainfall. The rain shadow to the east of the Palghat Gap has a significant impact on the agricultural and social development of this region.

What can be inferred from the passage?

A. The Palghat Gap is caused by high rainfall and heavy erodability.
B. The regions in Tamil Nadu and Kerala that are near the Palghat Gap are low-lying.
C. The low terrain of the Palghat Gap has a significant impact on rainfall distribution in the area.
D. Higher than average rainfall in the areas to the east of the Palghat Gap is a result of the rain shadow.
questions.answer: C. The low terrain of the Palghat Gap has a significant impact on rainfall distribution in the area.
questions.to_find: Inference that can be drawn from the passage about the Palghat Gap

understand.plain: The Palghat Gap is a roughly 30 km wide low-lying break in the Western Ghats. Because it is lower than the hills on either side, the South West monsoon winds can funnel through it, causing more rainfall on the Kerala (western) side and less rainfall in the Tamil Nadu (eastern) side — a rain shadow effect. The key causal link is: low terrain -> altered rainfall pattern -> agricultural and social impact.

keywords.explain: A break approximately 30 km wide in the Western Ghats, lower in elevation than the surrounding hills
keywords.example: A mountain pass or valley that allows wind and moisture to cross an otherwise tall barrier

keywords.explain: An area of reduced rainfall on the lee (downwind) side of a mountain range, because the mountains block moisture-carrying winds
keywords.example: Tamil Nadu areas east of the Gap receive less rainfall because moisture is blocked or channelled differently

keywords.explain: Seasonal winds from the southwest that bring heavy rainfall to the west coast of India
keywords.example: The monsoon season from June to September bringing rain to Kerala

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 200' width='500' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='200' class='bg' rx='10'/><polygon points='10,160 80,60 120,100 160,40 200,90 240,90 280,40 320,100 360,60 430,160' fill='#C8C4B0' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='190' y='90' width='60' height='70' class='hi'/><text x='215' y='175' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Gap</text><text x='80' y='180' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Kerala (W)</text><text x='380' y='180' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Tamil Nadu (E)</text><text x='40' y='130' class='tag ok'>More rain</text><text x='350' y='130' class='tag no'>Rain shadow</text><text x='250' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Western Ghats with Palghat Gap</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram of the Western Ghats cross-section showing the low-lying Palghat Gap, more rainfall on the Kerala western side, and a rain shadow on the Tamil Nadu eastern side

given.aim: Identify the correct inference based solely on the information provided in the passage

terms.meaning: The Gap is physically lower than the hills to its north and south, making it a break in the mountain barrier
terms.example: A valley or pass through a mountain range that allows airflow through
terms.connects: The low terrain is the direct cause of altered rainfall patterns — this is explicitly stated in the passage

terms.meaning: The pattern of how rain is spread across regions — Kerala gets more, Tamil Nadu gets less near the Gap
terms.example: Areas west of the Gap receive more monsoon rain; areas east receive less due to rain shadow
terms.connects: The passage directly states the Gap affects how rainfall is distributed across neighbouring regions

terms.meaning: Reduced rainfall on the side of a mountain barrier away from the oncoming wind
terms.example: East of the Palghat Gap has lower rainfall because the Gap's terrain channels moisture westward
terms.connects: The passage states the rain shadow to the east has significant impact — rain shadow means LESS rain, not more

given.plan: Check each option against the passage. A — the passage says the reasons for the Gap's formation are unclear, so we cannot infer it was caused by rainfall. B — the passage says the Gap is lower, not the neighbouring regions. C — the passage explicitly states the low terrain causes different rainfall on either side, matching option C. D — a rain shadow means LESS rainfall, not higher than average, so D is wrong.

steps.title: Establish what the passage explicitly states
steps.apply: The passage states: (1) the Gap is lower than surrounding terrain, (2) this results in Kerala neighbours getting more rainfall and Tamil Nadu neighbours getting less rainfall from the South West monsoon, and (3) the rain shadow to the east has a significant impact on agricultural and social development.
steps.note: These are the only facts we can use to draw inferences

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A — the passage says formation reasons are NOT clear, so we cannot infer high rainfall caused the Gap. B — the passage says the Gap is lower, not the neighbouring Tamil Nadu and Kerala regions. C — the passage directly links the low terrain of the Gap to differences in rainfall (more for Kerala, less for Tamil Nadu): this is a valid inference. D — a rain shadow means REDUCED rainfall to the east, not higher than average — option D is factually the opposite of what the passage says.
steps.note: Option D confuses rain shadow (less rain on the east) with increased rainfall

solution.result: C. The low terrain of the Palghat Gap has a significant impact on rainfall distribution in the area.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal passage-reading question; no mathematical formulae are required.
